title: Impact of daylight saving time on physical activity patterns
data_sources: NIH All of Us Research Program (Fitbit wearables)
All of Us Researcher Workbench (code repository)

data_scope.study_design: natural experiment: Mountain Time states (Colorado, New Mexico, Utah) vs. Arizona (no DST)
data_scope.wearable_measures: daily and hourly step counts; resting heart rate (intraday)
data_scope.sample_sizes_note: Subgroup Ns in Extended Data Tables: resting heart rate fall N=375-459, spring N=519-652; total cohort size not stated in available text
data_scope.phenotypes: morning walker, neutral walker, evening walker (data-driven)
data_scope.demographic_strata: age group, annual household income, sex
data_scope.built_environment_strata: sidewalks, public transit, bicycle facilities, recreational facilities
data_scope.data_access: gated via NIH All of Us Research Workbench (registration + ethics training + DUA required)

core_conclusion: DST transitions produce no net change in total daily steps; activity is reallocated across times of day — fall transitions increase morning steps by ~202 and reduce evening steps by ~180, spring transitions show the reverse. Effects vary substantially by chronotype phenotype and demographics, suggesting structural constraints (rigid work schedules, neighbourhood safety) limit adaptive flexibility for some populations. Resting heart rate shows subtle intraday mirroring but is clinically insignificant.
why_it_matters: First large-scale causal analysis of DST's effect using continuous wearables data; demonstrates that policy changes time of activity rather than total volume; reveals equity gaps by income/age/sex/chronotype; provides a replicable template for wearable-data causal policy evaluation.
why_nature: Health-policy translation at societal scale for an ongoing legislative debate; rigorous natural-experiment causal design with passive sensing; equity/heterogeneity dimension; open reproducible code on NIH platform.
added_value_to_my_research: GWR/MGWR spatialisation of DiD coefficients maps DST-effect variation across urban density and built-environment gradients
Wearables-based chronotype phenotyping extends my spatiotemporal KDE methods to circadian activity signatures
Linking activity reallocation to 2SFCA accessibility surfaces reveals compounded disadvantage of temporal + spatial barriers
Natural DiD template reusable for other time-policy experiments (park lighting, transit schedules) informing healthy-city analytics
EHR-wearables-GeoAI fusion model predicts which DST-disrupted populations then underutilise healthcare — telehealth targeting

related.title: Predicting schistosome transmission in rural Uganda using water contact data from wearable GPS devices

related.title: Benefit of physical activity initiatives for climate change mitigation and adaptation

figures.caption: Behavioural effects of DST transitions — DiD estimates for morning, evening, and full-day step counts showing activity reallocation across spring and fall transitions
Heterogeneity in behavioural responses to DST by physical activity phenotype — differential DiD effects for morning, neutral, and evening walkers
